transcript:
Right now you're watching a ring of pale gold particles breathing at the edge of a black canvas — quietly jittering, alive, intact. Then a word appears in the empty center: "thank." And that's the trigger. Watch what happens to the ring after that.

Look at the code and there's a variable literally called "dissolution radius" — it expands outward from the center, and each particle checks its own distance against it. The moment a particle falls inside that radius, it's marked "dissolved," and instead of scattering randomly, it spirals outward along its own angle — the artist's comment calls this "a kind of wave goodbye." Even the color math is deliberate: as each particle dissolves, its hue counts down from warm gold toward cooler tones, calculated in a function bluntly named "calculate gratitude intensity."

Notice, too, that this isn't a single ending — the code has a full reset built in, so once dissolution completes, the particles snap back to their origin points and start over. It's not a death. It's a loop that's been told to be grateful every single time it happens.
